peer_exemplars.reasoning: High openness and very high agreeableness, combined with strong anti-elitism and participatory democracy preferences, drive moderate support for citizen assemblies as a deliberative tool, though low conscientiousness tempers enthusiasm for sustained engagement.
High openness and populist people-centrism support participatory democracy, but low agreeableness and distrust in institutions temper enthusiasm.
High openness to experience and strong GAL-TAN pluralism and participatory democracy preference drive support, though low conscientiousness tempers absolute enthusiasm.

High GAL-TAN participatory democracy preference (83.73) and populist people-centrism (76.71) drive moderate support, but low openness (30.3) and high conscientiousness (99.56) temper enthusiasm for untested deliberative formats.

High openness, very low agreeableness, and strong GAL-TAN green/alternative/libertarian participatory democracy preference combine with ambivalent trust in formal institutions to produce mild support for an innovative, inclusive but potentially inefficient democratic mechanism.

Moderate support based on average openness, high agreeableness (cooperative deliberation), and GAL-TAN participatory democracy (44.23) tempered by low internal efficacy (42.73) and a pragmatic, conscientious preference for structured processes.

High openness and a strong GAL-TAN participatory democracy preference (46.17) combine with a libertarian skepticism of state control (14.75) to produce moderate support for citizen assemblies as a check on elite power.

High populist people-centrism (94.75) and anti-elitism (89.5) drive support for citizen-led deliberation, but low openness (20.68) and very high agreeableness (84.7) temper enthusiasm for unstructured participatory experiments.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high agreeableness and high trust in institutions, alongside a strong commitment to fairness and integrity, produce clear support for the norm of loser consent, though moderate system justification tempers a more maximal stance.
Strong support for democratic norms and high political trust align with endorsing loser consent, though low agreeableness may reduce full commitment.
Moderate support arises from very high agreeableness and fairness values, but weak external political efficacy and a competitive worldview create ambivalence.

Strong respect for rules (justice value) and high dutifulness (99.78) support procedural acceptance, yet high collective narcissism (94.21) and a dangerous worldview (79.16) create some reluctance to concede to out-groups.

Strong authoritarian submission (very low) and social dominance orientation (low) are countered by high competitive jungle worldview and moderate ethnic nationalism, leading to mild ambivalence about conceding to political opponents.

Mild opposition due to high right-wing authoritarianism (75.33), strong system justification (71.14), and a competitive-jungle worldview (83.69) that de-emphasizes procedural niceties when one's side loses.
High political trust (84.84) and civic nationalism (90.06) support a firm commitment to democratic norms, though a moderate authoritarian submission (28.81) tempers the score slightly.

Very high trust in electoral process (99.24) and strong fairness/reciprocity moral foundation (72.37) support loser consent, yet high social dominance orientation (89.57) introduces a slight reservation about fully empowering out-groups.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high trust in political institutions and high collective narcissism, combined with strong rejection of political violence, yield firm support for this foundational democratic norm, though not maximal due to moderate dangerous-worldview perceptions.
High authoritarian law-and-order preference and institutional trust strongly support peaceful transfers, despite low agreeableness.
Very high agreeableness and deference to legitimate authority provide a solid base, though authoritarian punitiveness and a dangerous-worldview add reservations about disruption.

Very high conscientiousness (99.56) and high trust in government (79.86) align with strong support for orderly transitions, though a history of coercive control exposure adds a slight wariness about institutional reliability.

High civic nationalism, very low RWA, and moderate trust in political institutions support a clear but not strong endorsement of the norm, while past exposure to political violence slightly tempers enthusiasm.

Clear support driven by very high conscientiousness (96.11), high dutifulness (83.07), and authoritarian submission (79.3) valuing order and stability, yet tempered by a dangerous worldview (70.04) that sees the process as fragile.
Very high trust in government (84.84) and strong law-and-order preference (60.94) align with a clear endorsement of peaceful transfers, reinforced by a high dangerous-worldview (67.73).

Extremely low traditional-authoritarian-nationalist values (1.93) and high anti-authoritarianism (5.14 RWA) strongly endorse peaceful transfers, though a criminal record for protest-related offenses adds a nuanced personal stake in orderly transitions.
